Title: Transposable Element-Mediated Structural Variation Drives Flower Colour Diversification in Camellia

Regulation_Genes.Source_Species: Camellia chrysanthoides / Camellia japonica ortholog context
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Sect. Chrysantha; Sect. Camellia; C. chrysanthoides; C. japonica
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher MYB60 expression suppresses anthocyanin biosynthesis in petals, contributing to yellow rather than red flower colour; a downstream TIR-type TE-associated SV increases MYB60 expression.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong negative regulator of anthocyanin accumulation associated with large-scale flower-colour divergence between yellow-flowered Sect. Chrysantha and red-flowered Sect. Camellia.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic pathway genes; specific direct targets not identified
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional repression associated with TE-mediated cis-regulatory activation of MYB60 expression
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: 240 bp TIR-type transposable element insertion 108 bp downstream of MYB60; lineage-specific structural variation in Sect. Chrysantha
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in yellow-flowered species than in red-flowered species
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in yellow petals / yellow-flowered species; lower in red-flowered species
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient overexpression in camellia petals; dual-luciferase assay in Nicotiana benthamiana
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: TE-mediated cis-regulatory structural variation; a TIR transposon insertion downstream positively regulates MYB60 expression
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome resequencing of 237 accessions; de novo genome assemblies of 11 genomes; petal transcriptomics from colour-divergent species; metabolite profiling by UPLC-MS; data availability stated as available on request, no accession provided
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Population SV association; petal transcriptome comparison; transient overexpression in camellia petals; anthocyanin quantification; dual-luciferase reporter assay
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to reduce anthocyanin accumulation and shift flower colour toward yellow/white through cis-regulatory editing or TE/SV marker-assisted selection; caution that direct downstream targets remain unresolved
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A 240 bp TIR-type TE insertion located 108 bp downstream of MYB60 is high-frequency in yellow-flowered Sect. Chrysantha and absent in predominantly red-flowered Sect. Camellia. 2. MYB60 expression is significantly higher in yellow-flowered species and negatively correlates with anthocyanin accumulation. 3. Transient expression of CcMYB60 in camellia petals suppresses anthocyanin biosynthesis. 4. Dual-luciferase assays show the TE-containing downstream fragment increases reporter activity, demonstrating positive cis-regulation of MYB60 by the SV.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper emphasizes TE-mediated homoeolog/ortholog expression divergence; MYB60 is the experimentally validated focal regulator in Results. Described in Discussion as orthologous to AtMYB60 reported as negative anthocyanin regulator in lettuce.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher MYB114 expression is associated with red petals and higher anthocyanin accumulation; a TIR-type TE insertion ~2.1 kb upstream is exclusive to red-flowered section species and likely elevates expression.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Candidate anthocyanin activator associated with red petal coloration, but not directly functionally validated in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic pathway genes; specific direct targets not identified
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Putative transcriptional activation with cis-regulatory modulation by upstream TE insertion
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: TIR-type transposable element insertion approximately 2.1 kb upstream in red-flowered species
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in red petals; lower in white petals
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in red-petaled Sect. Camellia species; lower in white-flowered Sect. Thea species
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparative petal transcriptomics across colour types
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: TE-mediated cis-regulatory structural variation upstream of the gene associated with increased expression
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome resequencing; graph-genome SV analysis; petal transcriptomics; metabolite profiling; no accession provided
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker or engineering target to enhance red pigmentation, though direct causal validation is still needed
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB114 is among genes upregulated in red petals. 2. Its coding sequence shows minimal differences across species, but expression differs markedly. 3. A TIR-type TE insertion about 2.1 kb upstream is found exclusively in red-flowered section species. 4. White-flowered Sect. Thea species lacking this SV show much lower MYB114 expression, implicating TE-mediated cis-regulation in anthocyanin activation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper cites prior report that MYB114 acts as an anthocyanin activator; in this study it is a strong candidate, not experimentally validated by overexpression or binding assays.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to yellow petal flavonol-related pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A 330 bp LTR/Copia insertion in intron 9 is associated with reduced MYB7 expression in Sect. Chrysantha; based on Arabidopsis orthology, MYB7 likely negatively regulates flavonol biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Candidate regulator associated with yellow-versus-red flavonoid divergence, but direct functional role in Camellia remains unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Flavonol biosynthetic pathway genes; specific direct targets not identified
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Likely represses flavonol biosynthetic genes based on Arabidopsis orthology
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Putative transcriptional repression modulated by intronic TE insertion that lowers MYB7 expression
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: 330 bp LTR/Copia-type TE insertion in ninth intron
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reduced in accessions carrying the intronic insertion
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in SV-carrying genotypes from Sect. Chrysantha compared with genotypes lacking the SV
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparative petal transcriptomics across colour types
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: TE-mediated intronic structural variation associated with lower expression; Discussion notes possible miR396-related mechanism but not proven
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome resequencing; graph-genome SV analysis; petal transcriptomics; flavonoid metabolite comparison; no accession provided
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modifying yellow/red flavonoid balance, but requires direct validation before deployment
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A Sect. Chrysantha-specific 330 bp LTR/Copia insertion occurs in intron 9 of a MYB7 ortholog. 2. Accessions carrying this SV show significantly reduced MYB7 expression. 3. Based on Arabidopsis evidence, MYB7 is a negative regulator of flavonol biosynthesis. 4. The SV is associated with yellow-versus-red petal flavonoid divergence, but mechanism remains unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussion notes the insertion may encode miR396, suggesting an additional regulatory layer; this is speculative and not validated in this paper.
